prompt:
Develop a LedgerRead Commerce & Compliance system for an offline-first publishing retailer that sells digital and physical titles, supports in-store operations, and enforces auditable governance. The React web interface serves three primary experiences: a high-performance reader for customers, a moderated community layer, and a staff-facing checkout console. Readers can open a title and navigate via table of contents, instantly switch between pagination and continuous scroll, and tune preferences including font family, font size (10–28 pt), line spacing (1.0–2.0), theme presets, night mode, and Simplified/Traditional Chinese; preferences persist per username and can be transferred to another device by importing/exporting an encrypted local “reading profile” file or optional LAN sync on the same network, with conflict resolution preferring the newest timestamp. Customers can favorite, subscribe to authors/series, rate on a 1–5 scale, post comments, reply in threads, and submit Q&A; they can also mute or block other users, and report content with a required category and free-text notes. Store clerks build carts by selecting SKUs, adjusting quantities, and receiving inline inventory feedback; the UI prompts bundle-building when complementary items exist, shows discounts and fee breakdown before submission, and forces a final “review total” step that revalidates price and stock to prevent tampering. Inventory managers and finance staff use the same UI to review supplier settlement status, discrepancy flags, and audit trails.

The backend uses NestJS to implement a decoupled REST/GraphQL API layer consumed by the React client over the local network or localhost, with PostgreSQL as the system of record for users, catalog, carts, inventory, costing, governance, and audit logs. Authentication is strictly offline with username/password (minimum 10 characters, at least one number and one symbol), salted hashing, 30-minute idle session timeout, and lockout after 5 failed attempts for 15 minutes; sensitive fields such as payment-plan notes and user identifiers are encrypted at rest, and comment text is stored with masking for blocked viewers. Content governance enforces a local sensitive-word dictionary, basic anti-spam rules (max 10 comments per minute per user, duplicate-post detection within 60 seconds), a moderation queue with role-based actions (hide, restore, remove, suspend), and immutable operation logs. Attendance modeling stores clock-in/out events, optional attached screenshot evidence files validated by file type and checksum, risk alerts triggered by rules (for example, “missing clock-out after 12 hours” or “evidence file mismatch”), and versioned rule definitions; each record links into a tamper-evident chain using hash signatures and timestamps, enabling audits without external services. Checkout and reconciliation enforce server-side inventory validation, moving-average inventory valuation, allocation of purchase cost plus freight/surcharges, supplier statement and invoice matching, payment plans with statuses, and discrepancy workflows that auto-flag when quantity differs by 2+ units or extended amount differs by more than $5.00. Recommendation endpoints run fully locally with precomputed Top-N and similar-item tables refreshed nightly, an in-memory cache with a 10-minute TTL, a 150 ms timeout fallback to best-sellers, and trace IDs recorded in PostgreSQL for troubleshooting and audit.
